speaker: interviewer
text: Once we go into the photographs and introducing the family, you're not really gonna have a chance to say anything much else. So if you-- you know, if you wanna spend-- you've got 15 minutes if you want it. It's up to you. Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just let me think a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let-- you know-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You up?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. This-- [BACKROUND NOISE] this is roll four, Esther Bem.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to add something. I said before that after the war I did not want to speak about the Holocaust, and I went, like, passionately after the business of living and experiencing. I wanted to make up for all those lost years fast. I wanted to learn and to study, and knew the music that was being played in the times when I wasn't around. And it was a discovery to-- to see that most of the population on this planet lived normally. That we were somewhere away like in-- on a different planet. And here, where people had lived normal lives. And I also want to say that I have, as I was passionately going after this business of living, I feel today such a gratitude to those people that saved us. And these are ordinary-- these were ordinary people that will never be in history books, that I unfortunately, don't even remember their names, and probably they're not alive anymore. And I probably wouldn't have even the emotional strength to find them. But I want to say in the era when goodness was very rare, they cultivated it. And they showed that-- that human decency and heroism, which was so rare in those times, they-- they did it for us. And I am aware today that to be heroic is so unpredictable. They probably wouldn't have known by themselves that they are going to behave the way that they did. They simply reacted toward despair with compassion. They didn't think of themselves. They didn't care what happens to them. And when you think we were not family. We were not even friends. We were strangers that fell from somewhere into their laps. And they never looked for excuses to say, well, we can't. I'm sorry. We have a young family. Never. And they were-- never made us feel, even, that we are intruding on them. And this is something that I want the post-Holocaust generation to know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that people have choices and they have proven it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your life? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- I think-- I cannot say what I would be like because I don't know. Probably, living in this isolation for so long made me a very social person. I like people. I like doing things for people. I always like to do-- them to receive because I received so much from them, and that is probably what's following me for the rest of my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also think that that moment in that-- in that City Hall made me very strong. That in moments of crisis, I reacted very bravely. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But again, I often even ask myself about me and other survivors, how did we remain normal? Because this really, for an adolescent, abnormal two years, living totally inside my head, not able-- being able to share with anybody my age, or close to my age, any of the thoughts. But what I would be like if I didn't, how can I know? Mrs. Bem, thank you for telling me your story. I'd like to stop the tape now. And we'll take a look at some of your family photos and then we'll bring on some of your family members.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] These are my two sisters. The older is Jelka. The younger is Vera. And that was the teddy bear that went with me through the war until I finished school in Yugoslavia, and then I left it when I came to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was eight here. That is 1938 in Zagreb. My sister Vera and I in the street very close to the house where we lived. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is my mother, Rebeca Svabenic, born Hessel. And this picture was taken right at the beginning of the war, in the '40s-- like '40 or '41. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my father, Arthur Svabenic. This picture was also taken '40 or '41. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is my sister, Jelka Svabenic, and this picture was taken about a year before she was executed. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This is my sister, Vera Svabenic, and the picture was taken in 1940. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] This is in Italy, as soon as we arrived in 1941. This was the only picture taken with me and my parents. And the place is Bassano del Grappa. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] From left to right, this is me, my friend Hari Dutka, Gina Taitatsak, and Iakov. This was taken right after the liberation. We are in Italy, in Venice. And these two guys have been in that truck that liberated us, that found us in that village, San Zenone. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] From left to right is Gina Taitatsak, Reli Pardo, and Esther, me. This is right after the liberation. We were lucky and happy, sitting in the gondola in Venice, having a good time. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This is in Zagreb after the war, when we were back and going to school. Left to right is my girlfriend, Maia Bramer, who is still alive and well and my good friend now. I am the next. The third, I don't know the name anymore. And the fourth is Anika Schpitser, who lives in Boston and we are friends. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my husband and I, Mirko Bem, right after we got married in 1950 in Tel Aviv. He was in the Army at that time. We came to Canada, and in 1975, he died. He was also a Holocaust survivor. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is my present husband, Victor Guth, whom I married in 1978, and this picture was taken in 1990. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is my grandson, Michael, on the left side in the gray suit. In the dark suit is my son-in-law, Simon Iakubovich. The boy in the blue blazer, his name is Alexander Iakubovich. My daughter, Liora, in her arms is Sean Iakubovich, and on her side is Jessica Iakubovich. These are my four grandchildren, my daughter and my son-in-law. And this picture was taken three years ago, in 1992 or '91. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] On the left is my daughter-in-law, Nikole Bem my son, Amir Bem and my grandson, Ariel Bem. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1994. I don't know where. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Behind me is my daughter Liora. On my left is my Jessica. On my right is Michael. Beside me is my son, Amir. And luckily, really, a combination of moments and all kinds of coincidences that are unimaginable, I survived and I have my descendents that I'm very proud of. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to say that growing up with-- with the stories of the Holocaust and hearing about people who are pictures in albums but no longer exist has been-- I'd have to say that it's been fundamental in shaping the way I look at the world and my views of-- of good and evil, and especially of defining myself as a Jew. And although I have this as a direct experience from growing up with my parents and hearing that, I fear that it's going to be lost, or it could be lost, in future generations. And that's why I'm-- I'm really thrilled with the process that we've gone through, that my mother has-- has spoken today and so we can show our children. Maybe pass on those experiences and allow them to be tangible and allow it to be part of their family history.
